| **Frustrations** | Hard to get first customers without an established reputation; existing experienced tradesmen have large personal networks he cannot yet compete with |
| **Needs** | A public profile where his qualifications and growing review history are visible; ability to set his own rate; ability to filter and choose jobs by location |

## **5.3 Functional Requirements (MoSCoW)**

Requirements were gathered from persona analysis, team domain research, and the competitive analysis. Table 7 presents the MoSCoW-prioritised functional requirements.

| ID | Requirement | Priority | Status |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Must Have** |  |  |  |
| FR01 | Users shall register as either Customer or Tradesman with role-specific fields | Must Have | Done |
| FR02 | Tradesman registration shall require trade type, hourly rate, and city | Must Have | Done |
| FR03 | Passwords shall be stored as bcrypt hashes (10 salt rounds) | Must Have | Done |
| FR04 | Users shall authenticate via email and password, receiving a JWT session token (7-day expiry) | Must Have | Done |
| FR05 | Sessions shall persist across browser refresh via localStorage token check | Must Have | Done |
| FR06 | Customers shall search tradesmen by trade, city, hourly rate range, minimum rating, and availability | Must Have | Done |
| FR07 | Customers shall view a tradesman's full profile: bio, pricing, ratings, and recent reviews | Must Have | Done |
| FR08 | Customers shall submit a job request to a specific tradesman with title, description, urgency, address, and optional offered fee | Must Have | Done |
| FR09 | Tradesmen shall view all incoming job requests assigned to them | Must Have | Done |
| FR10 | Tradesmen shall accept or decline pending job requests | Must Have | Done |
| FR11 | Customers shall mark an accepted job as completed | Must Have | Done |
| FR12 | Customers shall leave a 1–5 star rating and optional comment for completed jobs | Must Have | Done |
| FR13 | One review shall be permitted per job (enforced by DB UNIQUE constraint and application check) | Must Have | Done |
| FR14 | Tradesman avg\_rating and review\_count shall be automatically recalculated after each review | Must Have | Done |
| FR15 | Role-based access control: only customers create jobs; only tradesmen accept/decline | Must Have | Done |
| **Should Have** |  |  |  |
| FR16 | Search results shall support proximity sorting via Haversine formula when coordinates provided | Should Have | Done |
| FR17 | Search shall support sort by rating, price ascending/descending, and availability | Should Have | Done |
| FR18 | Tradesman list shall support pagination (LIMIT/OFFSET) | Should Have | Done |
| FR19 | My Jobs page shall show different content to customers vs tradesmen (role-conditional UI) | Should Have | Done |
| FR20 | Profile page shall display completed job history | Should Have | Done |
| **Could Have** |  |  |  |
| FR21 | Photo attachments on job requests (base64 storage implemented; no upload UI built) | Could Have | Partial |
| FR22 | Avatar/profile picture for tradesmen (column exists; no upload mechanism) | Could Have | Partial |
| **Won't Have (PoC Scope — Future Development)** |  |  |  |
| FR23 | Email/SMS notifications for job events | Won't Have | Not built |
| FR24 | In-app messaging between customer and tradesman | Won't Have | Not built |
| FR25 | In-app payment processing and escrow | Won't Have | Not built |
| FR26 | Admin panel for moderation and user management | Won't Have | Not built |
| FR27 | Tradesman profile self-editing after registration | Won't Have | Not built |

Table 7: MoSCoW Functional Requirements

## **5.4 Non-Functional Requirements**

| ID | Category | Requirement | Implementation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| NFR01 | Security | All protected routes require a valid JWT bearer token | middleware/auth.js |
| NFR02 | Security | Passwords stored as bcrypt hashes (10 salt rounds) | bcryptjs |
| NFR03 | Security | Helmet middleware sets security HTTP headers | helmet() in server.js |
| NFR04 | Security | CORS configured to allow frontend origin only | cors() in server.js |
| NFR05 | Performance | SQLite WAL mode for concurrent read performance | PRAGMA WAL |
| NFR06 | Performance | Denormalised avg\_rating avoids aggregate joins on every search | tradesman\_profiles |
| NFR07 | Reliability | Foreign keys enforced at DB level | PRAGMA FK ON |
| NFR08 | Usability | Responsive design — UI adapts at 700px breakpoint | CSS Grid \+ media queries |
| NFR09 | Usability | All async operations show loading spinners | useState(loading) |
| NFR10 | Scalability | Search supports LIMIT/OFFSET pagination | tradesmensController |

Table 8: Non-Functional Requirements

# **6\. Design**

## **6.1 House Style**

The MasterLink visual identity is built around a primary colour palette of deep navy blue (\#1F3864) and medium blue (\#2E75B6), with white backgrounds and light blue accents for surfaces. This palette was chosen deliberately: blue conveys trust, reliability, and professionalism — values essential to a platform whose entire proposition rests on helping users find people they can trust in their home (Elliot & Maier, 2014). Status colours are semantic and consistent throughout the application: green indicates positive / available / accepted states; orange indicates pending / awaiting action; red indicates declined or unavailable; blue indicates completed work.

Typography uses the system font stack (-apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', sans-serif), ensuring that the application renders with optimal legibility on any device and operating system without requiring a custom font download. This is particularly important for users on slower mobile connections. Font sizes follow a clear hierarchy: large page headings at 32px, section headings at 20–24px, and body text at 14–16px. All interactive elements (buttons, links, status badges) have clearly visible hover and active states.

The responsive breakpoint at 700px causes the filter sidebar to stack above the search results grid, maintaining usability on mobile screens without requiring a separate mobile codebase. Cards use a consistent border-radius of 12px and box-shadow for visual depth.

## **6.2 System Architecture (Figure A)**

![][image1]

*Figure A — System Architecture Diagram (Three-Tier)*

![][image1]

*Figure A — System Architecture Diagram (Three-Tier)*

MasterLink follows a classic three-tier architecture. The presentation layer is a React Single-Page Application (SPA) running in the browser, built and bundled by Vite. It communicates with the application layer — an Express.js REST API running on Node.js (port 3000\) — exclusively via JSON HTTP requests, with JWTs included in the Authorization header for authenticated routes. The application layer in turn communicates with the data layer — a SQLite database file — via the better-sqlite3 synchronous driver.

The three tiers are physically separated: the frontend is served by Vite's development server (port 5173\) and in production would be deployed to a CDN (e.g. Vercel); the backend is deployed to a cloud provider (e.g. Railway); and the database would be migrated to a hosted PostgreSQL instance for production. This separation enables independent scaling and deployment of each tier.

The middleware chain within Express handles requests in the following sequence: incoming request → Helmet (security headers) → CORS → JSON body parser → JWT verifyToken middleware → Route handler → Controller → SQLite query → JSON response → Global error handler.

![][image2]

*Figure B — Request/Response Flow Sequence Diagram*

The repository is organised as a monorepo with two top-level packages: /backend (the Express API) and /web (the React application), each with their own package.json and node\_modules.

## **6.3 Use Case Diagram (Figure F)**

![][image3]

*Figure F — Use Case Diagram*

The use case diagram identifies two actor types — Customer and Tradesman — interacting with the MasterLink system. Customer use cases include: Register, Login, Search Tradesmen, Apply Filters and Sort, View Tradesman Profile, Submit Job Request, View My Jobs (sent), Mark Job Complete, Leave Review, and View Profile. Tradesman use cases include: Register, Login, View Incoming Jobs, Accept Job Request, Decline Job Request, and View Profile. Register, Login, and View Profile are shared between both actors.

## **6.4 Activity Diagrams**

### **6.4.1 Customer Booking Flow (Figure G)**

![][image4]

*Figure G — Customer Booking Activity Diagram*

![][image5]

*Figure G — Customer Booking Activity Diagram*

The customer booking activity diagram traces the full flow from a new user's first visit to the post-job review. The user visits the landing page (if unauthenticated) and registers as a customer. After registration, they are directed to the search page, where they apply filters to find a suitable tradesman. They click View Profile to assess the tradesman's bio, pricing, and reviews, then click Request Service to open the job request form. After completing the form and submitting it, the job appears in My Jobs with status PENDING. When the tradesman accepts, status changes to ACCEPTED. After the physical work is completed, the customer clicks Mark as Completed. A review modal then prompts them to rate the tradesman (1–5 stars) and optionally write a comment. Submitting the review recalculates the tradesman's average rating and review count.

### **6.4.2 Tradesman Job Management Flow (Figure G2)**

The tradesman activity flow begins with login. On the My Jobs page, the tradesman sees a list of incoming requests ordered by creation date. For each PENDING job, they can review the job details (title, description, address, urgency, offered fee, customer name) and then either accept or decline. Accepted jobs remain visible in a separate section. Once the customer marks the job complete, the tradesman's profile is updated with the new review and rating.

## **6.5 Job Status State Machine (Figure D)**

![][image6]

*Figure D — Job Status State Machine*

Job requests in MasterLink progress through a well-defined state machine. A job is created with status PENDING. The tradesman can then transition it to ACCEPTED or DECLINED. DECLINED is a terminal state — the job cannot be reactivated. From ACCEPTED, only the customer can trigger the transition to COMPLETED by clicking 'Mark as Complete'. COMPLETED is also a terminal state, and it is the only state from which a review can be submitted. This state machine is enforced both at the API level (the PATCH /api/jobs/:id/status endpoint validates transitions and ownership) and at the database level (a CHECK constraint limits the status column to the four valid values).

## **6.6 Navigation Map (Figure E)**

![][image7]

*Figure E — Application Navigation Map*

The application has eight routes, organised into public and protected groups. Public routes (no authentication required): / (Landing Page), /login (Login Page), /register (Register Page). Protected routes (require valid JWT; redirect to /login if absent): /search (Search Page), /tradesman/:id (Tradesman Detail Page), /job-request/:tradesmanId (Job Request Page), /my-jobs (My Jobs Page), /profile (Profile Page).

Navigation between routes: the Landing Page links to Login and Register. Login and Register both redirect to Search on success. From Search, users click View Profile to reach Tradesman Detail. From Tradesman Detail, clicking Request Service navigates to Job Request. From Job Request, submitting navigates to My Jobs. The persistent Navbar (visible on all authenticated pages) provides direct links to Search, My Jobs, and Profile. The Profile page includes a Logout action that clears the session and returns the user to the Landing Page. A catch-all route (\*) redirects to Landing Page for unknown paths.

## **6.7 Database Design**

### **6.7.1 Normalisation**

The database schema was designed to at least Third Normal Form (3NF). The initial unnormalised data structure contained repeated information about users mixed with profile details, job information, and review data in a conceptual flat record. Applying First Normal Form (1NF) eliminated repeating groups. Applying Second Normal Form (2NF) removed partial dependencies on composite keys. Third Normal Form (3NF) removed transitive dependencies, resulting in four clean tables: users, tradesman\_profiles, job\_requests, and reviews.

One intentional departure from strict normalisation is the denormalisation of avg\_rating and review\_count into tradesman\_profiles. This is a performance optimisation: computing these values via an AVG() / COUNT() aggregate query at search time — when dozens of tradesmen may be returned — is expensive. Instead, these values are recalculated and stored immediately after each review insertion (see Section 7.1.4). This is a well-established pattern in read-heavy marketplace systems.

### **6.7.2 Entity-Relationship Diagram (Figure C)**

![][image8]

*Figure C — Entity-Relationship Diagram (ERD)*

The ERD contains four entities with the following relationships:

* users (1) → (0..1) tradesman\_profiles — one user optionally has one tradesman profile (only users with role='tradesman' have a profile row)

* users \[customer\] (1) → (0..\*) job\_requests via customer\_id — one customer creates many job requests

* users \[tradesman\] (1) → (0..\*) job\_requests via tradesman\_id — one tradesman receives many job requests

* job\_requests (1) → (0..1) reviews — one job optionally generates one review (UNIQUE constraint on reviews.job\_id)

* users (1) → (0..\*) reviews via reviewer\_id — one user writes many reviews

* users (1) → (0..\*) reviews via reviewee\_id — one user receives many reviews

### **6.7.3 Data Dictionaries**

Table 9 presents the data dictionary for the four database tables.

**users table**

| Column | Type | Constraints | Nullable | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| id | INTEGER | PK, AUTOINCREMENT | No | Unique user identifier |
| name | TEXT | NOT NULL | No | Full display name |
| email | TEXT | UNIQUE, NOT NULL | No | Login credential; globally unique |
| password\_hash | TEXT | NOT NULL | No | bcrypt hash of user password |
| role | TEXT | NOT NULL, CHECK | No | 'customer' or 'tradesman' |
| avatar\_url | TEXT | — | Yes | Optional profile picture URL |
| created\_at | DATETIME | DEFAULT NOW | No | Account creation timestamp |

**tradesman\_profiles table**

| Column | Type | Constraints | Nullable | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| user\_id | INTEGER | NOT NULL, UNIQUE, FK | No | Links to users.id; CASCADE DELETE |
| trade | TEXT | NOT NULL | No | Plumber / Electrician / etc. |
| hourly\_rate | REAL | NOT NULL | No | Charge per hour |
| call\_out\_fee | REAL | DEFAULT 50 | Yes | Fixed site visit fee |
| city | TEXT | NOT NULL | No | Operating city |
| latitude / longitude | REAL | — | Yes | GPS coordinates for proximity sort |
| avg\_rating | REAL | DEFAULT 0 | No | Denormalised average rating |
| review\_count | INTEGER | DEFAULT 0 | No | Denormalised review count |
| is\_available | INTEGER | DEFAULT 1 | No | 1 \= available, 0 \= unavailable |
| years\_experience | INTEGER | DEFAULT 1 | No | Years in trade |

**job\_requests table**

| Column | Type | Constraints | Nullable | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| customer\_id | INTEGER | NOT NULL, FK | No | Customer who created the request |
| tradesman\_id | INTEGER | NOT NULL, FK | No | Tradesman the request is directed to |
| title | TEXT | NOT NULL | No | Short description of work |
| urgency | TEXT | CHECK | No | 'emergency' or 'flexible' |
| status | TEXT | CHECK, DEFAULT 'pending' | No | pending/accepted/declined/completed |
| offered\_fee | REAL | — | Yes | Customer's proposed payment amount |

**reviews table**

| Column | Type | Constraints | Nullable | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| job\_id | INTEGER | NOT NULL, UNIQUE, FK | No | Linked job; UNIQUE \= one review per job |
| reviewer\_id | INTEGER | NOT NULL, FK | No | Customer who wrote the review |
| reviewee\_id | INTEGER | NOT NULL, FK | No | Tradesman being reviewed |
| rating | INTEGER | CHECK(1-5), NOT NULL | No | Star rating from 1 (worst) to 5 (best) |
| comment | TEXT | — | Yes | Optional written feedback |

Table 9: Data Dictionaries

# **7\. Implementation**

Before the frontend could be built, the database schema and backend API had to be implemented and verified. This approach — data layer first, then application layer, then presentation layer — ensured that each tier was stable and testable independently before the next was added.

## **7.1 Database Implementation**

### **7.1.1 Schema & Configuration**

The database is initialised by backend/db/database.js, which runs two critical PRAGMA statements on every connection before any other query:

* PRAGMA journal\_mode \= WAL — enables Write-Ahead Logging mode, which allows concurrent reads during writes and significantly improves performance under modest multi-user loads.

* PRAGMA foreign\_keys \= ON — SQLite does not enforce foreign key constraints by default; this pragma enables them, providing database-level referential integrity.

The schema is defined in backend/db/schema.sql and contains four CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS statements. The use of IF NOT EXISTS ensures that re-running the schema on an existing database does not destroy data. The schema is loaded and executed on server startup if the tables do not yet exist.

### **7.1.2 Tables**

Nine tables were planned initially; the final implemented schema contains four tables. Each table was created with appropriate data types, constraints, and default values as specified in the data dictionaries (Section 6.7.3).

The job\_requests table is the most complex, containing 13 fields of mixed types including INTEGER, TEXT, REAL, DATETIME, and a CHECK constraint on the status field limiting it to the four valid lifecycle values: 'pending', 'accepted', 'declined', 'completed'. A DEFAULT of 'pending' is also set, ensuring every newly created job begins in the correct initial state without requiring the application to explicitly set this value. A similar CHECK constraint on the urgency field limits it to 'emergency' or 'flexible'.

The reviews table enforces a UNIQUE constraint on job\_id, which is the primary mechanism preventing duplicate reviews. This constraint operates at the database level, providing a backstop even if application-level checks were bypassed.

### **7.1.3 Relationships**

All eleven relationships between the four tables are implemented as FOREIGN KEY constraints. The tradesman\_profiles.user\_id foreign key includes ON DELETE CASCADE, ensuring that deleting a user account also removes their tradesman profile. All other foreign keys use the default SQLite behaviour (RESTRICT), preventing deletion of a user while they have associated job requests or reviews.

### **7.1.4 Rating Recalculation Logic**

After every review insertion, the following logic in reviewsController.js recalculates and persists the tradesman's aggregate rating statistics:

SELECT AVG(rating) as avg, COUNT(\*) as cnt FROM reviews WHERE reviewee\_id \= ?

UPDATE tradesman\_profiles SET avg\_rating \= ROUND(avg, 1), review\_count \= cnt WHERE user\_id \= ?

The average is rounded to one decimal place before storage (e.g. 4.75 rounds to 4.8). This produces consistent, clean values for display without requiring rounding in every query or in the frontend.

### **7.1.5 Seed Data**

The seed script (backend/db/seed.js) populates the database with realistic demo data. All accounts use the password 'password123' (bcrypt-hashed). The seed creates 6 customer accounts and 12 tradesman accounts covering all 6 supported trades (Plumber, Electrician, Carpenter, Painter, Tiler, Builder) across both cities (Dushanbe and Khujand). Each tradesman has realistic profile data: hourly rates, years of experience, availability flags, real GPS coordinates, and review histories. The seed also creates 19 historical completed jobs and 4 active jobs (in pending/accepted states), plus 18 reviews. This rich demo dataset makes the platform immediately usable for demonstration purposes.

| Name | Trade | City | Rating | Reviews | Email |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Rustam Nazarov | Plumber | Dushanbe | 4.8 | 87 | rustam.n@example.com |
| Firuz Raximov | Electrician | Dushanbe | 4.9 | 112 | firuz.r@example.com |
| Bahrom Karimov | Carpenter | Dushanbe | 4.7 | 63 | bahrom.k@example.com |
| Nilufar Rahimova | Painter | Dushanbe | 4.6 | 51 | nilufar.r@example.com |
| Nodir Tursunov | Tiler | Dushanbe | 4.7 | 38 | nodir.t@example.com |
| Umed Sultonov | Plumber | Khujand | 4.5 | 46 | umed.s@example.com |

Table 10: Selected Seed Tradesman Accounts (full password: password123)

## **7.2 Backend API Implementation**

### **7.2.1 Server Setup**

The Express application entry point (server.js) configures the middleware stack and mounts the four route modules. Middleware applied globally (to every request): helmet() for security headers, cors() for cross-origin request handling, and express.json({ limit: '10mb' }) for JSON body parsing (the 10MB limit accommodates base64-encoded photo data). Routes are mounted at /api/auth, /api/tradesmen, /api/jobs, and /api/reviews. A global error handler at the bottom of the middleware stack catches any unhandled errors and returns a standardised JSON error response. A health check endpoint GET /health returns { status: 'ok' } for deployment readiness checks.

### **7.2.2 Authentication — Registration & Login**

The registration controller (authController.js) implements the following logic sequence: validate that name, email, password, and role are present in the request body; validate that role is either 'customer' or 'tradesman'; if role is 'tradesman', validate that trade, hourly\_rate, and city are also present; query the database to check that no existing user has the same email (returning HTTP 409 Conflict if a duplicate is found); hash the password using bcryptjs with 10 salt rounds; insert the new user row into the users table; if role is 'tradesman', insert a corresponding row into tradesman\_profiles; sign a JWT with the payload { userId, role, email } and a 7-day expiry; return HTTP 201 with the token and the safe user object (explicitly excluding password\_hash).

The login controller queries the users table by email; returns HTTP 401 if not found; uses bcrypt.compareSync to validate the password against the stored hash; returns HTTP 401 if the password does not match; otherwise signs and returns a fresh JWT.

The GET /api/auth/me endpoint verifies the JWT (via the verifyToken middleware) and fetches the current user's data from the database. If the user is a tradesman, it also fetches the tradesman\_profiles row. This endpoint is called on application load to restore the session state after a page refresh.

### **7.2.3 Tradesman Search & Geo-Proximity**

The tradesman list controller builds a parameterised SQL query dynamically, starting with a base JOIN between tradesman\_profiles and users, then conditionally appending WHERE clauses based on which query parameters are present:

* AND LOWER(tp.trade) \= LOWER(?) when trade parameter is provided

* AND LOWER(tp.city) LIKE LOWER(?) with wildcard wrapping for city

* AND tp.hourly\_rate \>= ? for minimum rate

* AND tp.hourly\_rate \<= ? for maximum rate

* AND tp.avg\_rating \>= ? for minimum rating

* AND tp.is\_available \= 1 when available=true is passed

* LIMIT ? OFFSET ? always applied for pagination

If latitude and longitude coordinates are provided in the query string, the Haversine formula is applied in JavaScript after the database query to compute the great-circle distance (in kilometres) between the user's location and each tradesman's stored coordinates. The resulting array is then re-sorted in ascending distance order, with tradesmen lacking coordinates placed last. This approach — querying first, sorting afterwards — is appropriate for the PoC scale; at production scale, a spatial index (PostGIS, or SQLite's spatialite extension) would be more efficient.

The Haversine formula implementation uses Earth's mean radius of 6,371 km. For the distances relevant to Tajikistan's two major cities (approximately 300 km apart), this approximation is sufficiently accurate.

### **7.2.4 Job Request Management**

Three endpoints handle job request management. POST /api/jobs (customer role only) validates that the target tradesman\_id exists and belongs to a user with role='tradesman', then inserts the job request with status='pending'. GET /api/jobs/mine serves both roles: for customers, it returns jobs where customer\_id matches the authenticated user, joined with tradesman name and trade; for tradesmen, it returns jobs where tradesman\_id matches, joined with customer name. Both result sets are ordered by creation date descending.

PATCH /api/jobs/:id/status handles all status transitions. The controller fetches the job, then verifies that the requesting user is either the customer\_id or tradesman\_id of that specific job (returning HTTP 403 if neither). It then updates the status field to the requested value. The UI enforces role-appropriate actions — tradesmen see Accept and Decline buttons; customers see Mark Complete — but the API does not enforce this separation beyond ownership checks, providing flexibility for future feature additions.

### **7.2.5 Review Creation & Rating Update**

POST /api/reviews performs the following validation chain: both job\_id and rating must be present; rating must be an integer between 1 and 5 (HTTP 400 otherwise); the job must exist and must be owned by the authenticated customer (HTTP 403 otherwise); the job status must be 'completed' (HTTP 400 if not — it is not possible to review an ongoing or declined job); no existing review for this job\_id must exist (HTTP 409 if duplicate). After passing all checks, the review row is inserted, and the recalculation query updates avg\_rating and review\_count in tradesman\_profiles. HTTP 201 is returned with the new review object.

### **7.2.6 JWT Middleware**

The verifyToken middleware in backend/middleware/auth.js extracts the Bearer token from the Authorization header, verifies it against JWT\_SECRET using the HS256 algorithm, and attaches the decoded payload as req.user \= { userId, role, email } for downstream route handlers to consume. A missing, malformed, or expired token returns HTTP 401 Unauthorized. A requireRole(role) helper is also exported for use by specific routes where a particular role must be enforced at the route level (e.g., the POST /api/jobs route applies requireRole('customer')).

### **7.2.7 Complete API Endpoint Reference**

| Method \+ Path | Auth | Status | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Authentication (/api/auth)** |  |  |  |
| POST /register | None | 201 / 409 | Register new user; returns JWT |
| POST /login | None | 200 / 401 | Login; returns JWT |
| GET /me | Bearer JWT | 200 / 401 | Fetch current user \+ optional tradesman profile |
| **Tradesmen (/api/tradesmen)** |  |  |  |
| GET / | None (public) | 200 | Search \+ filter \+ paginate tradesmen |
| GET /:id | None (public) | 200 / 404 | Full tradesman profile \+ 5 most recent reviews |
| **Jobs (/api/jobs)** |  |  |  |
| POST / | Bearer JWT, customer role | 201 / 400/403 | Create job request directed at a tradesman |
| GET /mine | Bearer JWT, both roles | 200 | List jobs for current user (role-conditional query) |
| PATCH /:id/status | Bearer JWT, ownership check | 200 / 403/404 | Update job status (accept/decline/complete) |
| **Reviews (/api/reviews)** |  |  |  |
| POST / | Bearer JWT, customer (job ownership) | 201 / 400/403/409 | Create review; recalculate tradesman rating |

Table 11: Complete API Endpoint Reference

## **7.3 Frontend Implementation**

### **7.3.1 Application Entry & Routing**

The React application entry point is web/src/main.jsx, which renders the App component into the \#root DOM element. App.jsx configures the full routing structure using React Router DOM v7. The router is wrapped in an AuthProvider — a React context that exposes global authentication state (user object, role, loading flag) to all child components. A Navbar component, also inside the AuthProvider, is rendered outside the Routes element so it persists across all pages.

Routes are divided into two categories. Public routes — /, /login, /register — are accessible without authentication. Protected routes — /search, /tradesman/:id, /job-request/:tradesmanId, /my-jobs, /profile — are wrapped in a ProtectedRoute component that checks for an authenticated user. If the user is not authenticated (and the loading check has completed), ProtectedRoute redirects to /login. A catch-all route (\*) redirects to the root. Authenticated users visiting the root (/) are automatically redirected to /search.

### **7.3.2 Authentication Context & Session Persistence**

AuthContext.jsx provides global authentication state via React's Context API. On application mount, the context checks localStorage for an existing token. If found, it calls GET /api/auth/me to validate the token server-side and hydrate the user state. This approach handles browser refresh without losing the session — the user remains logged in as long as their 7-day JWT has not expired.

The Axios instance in web/src/api/client.js has two interceptors. The request interceptor reads the token from localStorage and attaches it as the Authorization: Bearer header to every outgoing API request. The response interceptor listens for HTTP 401 responses — if one is received, it clears localStorage and redirects the browser to /login, handling expired or invalidated tokens transparently.

### **7.3.3 Page-by-Page Implementation**

Landing Page (/): Shown only to unauthenticated users — authenticated users are redirected to /search automatically. Contains a hero section with tagline, a four-step process overview (Search → Request → Accept → Get Fixed), clickable trade category pills, and CTA buttons to Register and Login.

Login Page (/login): Email and password input fields with inline validation error display. On successful login, the JWT and user object are stored via AuthContext.login() and the user is redirected to /search. Demo credentials are displayed on the page for evaluation convenience.

Register Page (/register): A single form covering all registration fields. Role selection (Customer / Tradesman) is implemented as two button-style toggles. When Tradesman is selected, three additional fields appear conditionally: Trade (dropdown with 6 options), City (text input), and Hourly Rate (number input). On submission, the appropriate POST /api/auth/register payload is assembled and sent. On success, the user is redirected to /search.

Search Page (/search): The most feature-rich page. A sidebar (collapsible below 700px) contains six filter controls: name search (text), city (text), trade (dropdown), minimum/maximum hourly rate (number inputs), minimum rating (dropdown: Any / 3+ / 4+ / 4.5+), and available only (checkbox). Four sort options are available: Highest Rated, Price: Low to High, Price: High to Low, and Available First. Filter and sort state is persisted in the URL as query parameters, making searches bookmarkable and shareable. Search results are displayed as a responsive grid of TradesmanCard components, each showing the tradesman's avatar (initials if no photo), name, trade badge, city, rating (with star display), review count, hourly rate, and availability badge. Loading state is shown while data is fetching; an empty state message appears when no results match the filters.

Tradesman Detail Page (/tradesman/:id): Fetches and displays the full tradesman profile on mount via GET /api/tradesmen/:id. Sections include: a profile card with avatar, name, trade badge, city, availability badge, years of experience, average rating, and review count; a pricing card showing hourly rate and call-out fee; a bio section; and a reviews section listing up to 5 recent reviews with reviewer name, star rating, date, and comment. A prominent Request Service CTA button navigates to the Job Request page, passing tradesman data via React Router's location state.

Job Request Page (/job-request/:tradesmanId): A form for customers to compose and submit a job request. Fields: job title (text), description (textarea), address (text), urgency level (toggle: Flexible / Emergency), scheduled date (date picker), and offered fee (number, optional). The tradesman's name and trade are displayed at the top of the form as context. On submission, POST /api/jobs is called; on success, the user is navigated to /my-jobs.

My Jobs Page (/my-jobs): Displays differently based on the authenticated user's role. Customers see a list of all job requests they have sent, grouped by status (pending, accepted, declined, completed). Each accepted job has a Mark as Complete button that triggers PATCH /api/jobs/:id/status { status: 'completed' }. After marking complete, a Review Modal appears: a 1–5 star picker (interactive click-to-rate) and an optional comment textarea. Submitting the review calls POST /api/reviews. Tradesmen see a list of all incoming job requests assigned to them, with Accept and Decline buttons for pending jobs.

Profile Page (/profile): Displays the current user's account information and, for tradesmen, their full professional profile details. Shows the history of completed jobs. Includes a Logout button that calls AuthContext.logout() and redirects to the landing page.

### **7.3.4 Reusable Components**

![][image9]

*Figure H — React Component Hierarchy*

Two shared components are used across multiple pages. The Navbar component renders on all authenticated pages, providing navigation links and a Logout action. It reads from AuthContext to display role-appropriate links (for example, the My Jobs link is present for both roles, but its behaviour differs). The TradesmanCard component renders a standardised summary card for each tradesman in search results. It accepts a tradesman object as a prop and renders a consistent, clickable card linking to the Tradesman Detail page.

## **7.4 Technical Challenges**

Several technical challenges arose during implementation that are worth documenting as learning points.

Dynamic SQL query construction for search filtering was the most complex backend challenge. Building a safe parameterised query where the number of WHERE clauses varies based on which filters are active required careful management of the query string and the parameters array. Early implementations suffered from off-by-one errors in the parameters array when certain filter combinations were used — this was resolved by using a clauses array and a params array that are pushed to simultaneously and then joined at query execution.

Session hydration on page refresh was a tricky frontend challenge. The initial implementation caused a brief flash of the login page for authenticated users on refresh, because the ProtectedRoute component rendered before the AuthContext had completed the GET /api/auth/me verification. This was resolved by introducing a loading boolean state in AuthContext — ProtectedRoute renders a loading spinner while loading is true and only redirects to login once loading is false and user is null.

The Haversine proximity sort initially produced incorrect results due to a degrees-to-radians conversion error — the formula requires coordinates in radians, but the stored values are in degrees. The conversion (multiply by Math.PI / 180\) was missing in the first implementation, causing very large (incorrect) distance values. This was caught during manual testing of the proximity sort feature.

Connecting the job status PATCH endpoint to both customer and tradesman views in a single endpoint while maintaining correct ownership validation required careful conditional logic. The initial implementation incorrectly blocked tradesmen from updating job status because the ownership check was testing customer\_id before checking tradesman\_id. The fix was to check ownership against both fields and accept the request if either matches.

# **8\. Testing**

## **8.1 Test Plan and Results**

The following test plan was carried out systematically after the completion of each sprint and as a comprehensive end-to-end test after Sprint 7\. Tests cover all implemented functional requirements and key non-functional requirements. All testing was conducted manually using the application running locally with the seed database populated. Table 12 records the full test results.

| ID | Test Summary | Pre-conditions | Procedure | Expected Result | Result |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Registration & Login** |  |  |  |  |  |
| T01 | Register as customer with valid details | Not logged in | Fill name, email, password; select Customer; submit | Account created; token stored; redirected to /search |  |
| T02 | Register as tradesman with valid details | Not logged in | Fill name, email, password; select Tradesman; fill trade, city, rate; submit | Account created; tradesman\_profile row created; token stored; redirected to /search |  |
| T03 | Register with existing email | Not logged in | Enter email already in seed data; submit | HTTP 409 error message displayed; form not submitted |  |
| T04 | Register without required fields (customer) | Not logged in | Leave name blank; submit | Error message displayed; form not submitted |  |
| T05 | Register as tradesman without trade field | Not logged in | Select Tradesman; leave trade blank; submit | Error message displayed; form not submitted |  |
| T06 | Login with valid credentials | Existing account | Enter sarvinoz@example.com / password123; submit | Token stored; user state set; redirected to /search |  |
| T07 | Login with wrong password | Existing account | Enter correct email, wrong password; submit | HTTP 401 error message displayed; not logged in |  |
| T08 | Session persists after page refresh | Logged in | Refresh browser page | User remains logged in; no redirect to /login |  |
| T09 | Logout clears session | Logged in | Click Logout on Profile page | Token removed; user state null; redirected to Landing Page |  |
| **Search & Discovery** |  |  |  |  |  |
| T10 | Search without filters returns all tradesmen | Logged in | Navigate to /search; no filters applied | All 12 seed tradesmen displayed in results grid |  |
| T11 | Filter by trade — Plumber | Logged in | Select Plumber from trade dropdown | Only plumbers returned (Rustam, Umed, Davron) |  |
| T12 | Filter by city — Khujand | Logged in | Enter 'Khujand' in city filter | Only Khujand tradesmen returned |  |
| T13 | Filter by availability only | Logged in | Check 'Available only' | Only tradesmen with is\_available=1 returned |  |
| T14 | Filter by minimum rating 4.5+ | Logged in | Select 4.5+ from rating dropdown | Only tradesmen with avg\_rating \>= 4.5 returned |  |
| T15 | Sort by highest rating | Logged in | Select 'Highest Rated' sort option | Results ordered descending by avg\_rating |  |
| T16 | Sort by price: low to high | Logged in | Select 'Price: Low to High' | Results ordered ascending by hourly\_rate |  |
| T17 | Combine trade \+ city filter | Logged in | Select Electrician \+ enter Dushanbe | Only Dushanbe electricians shown (Firuz, Sherzod excluded) |  |
| T18 | View tradesman profile from search | Logged in | Click View Profile on a tradesman card | Tradesman Detail page loads; all profile data correct; reviews displayed |  |
| **Job Request Flow** |  |  |  |  |  |
| T19 | Customer submits job request | Logged in as customer | Navigate to tradesman detail; click Request Service; fill form; submit | Job created with status 'pending'; visible in My Jobs |  |
| T20 | Customer submits job request — title required | Logged in as customer | Open job request form; leave title blank; submit | Error displayed; form not submitted |  |
| T21 | Tradesman cannot submit job request | Logged in as tradesman | Attempt POST /api/jobs via direct API call | HTTP 403 Forbidden returned |  |
| T22 | Tradesman sees pending jobs in My Jobs | Logged in as tradesman | Navigate to /my-jobs | All incoming pending jobs listed with Accept/Decline buttons |  |
| T23 | Tradesman accepts job request | Logged in as tradesman | Click Accept on a pending job | Job status changes to 'accepted'; confirmation displayed |  |
| T24 | Tradesman declines job request | Logged in as tradesman | Click Decline on a pending job | Job status changes to 'declined'; job still visible in history |  |
| T25 | Customer marks accepted job as complete | Logged in as customer | Click Mark as Complete on an accepted job | Job status changes to 'completed'; review modal appears |  |
| T26 | Customer cannot complete a pending job | Logged in as customer | Attempt PATCH /api/jobs/:id/status {completed} on pending job | Status not changed; error returned (only customer can complete accepted) |  |
| T27 | Non-owner cannot modify job status | Logged in as wrong user | Attempt PATCH on a job not belonging to the user | HTTP 403 Forbidden returned |  |
| **Reviews & Ratings** |  |  |  |  |  |
| T28 | Customer submits review after job completion | Logged in as customer | Mark job as complete; fill review modal; submit 5 stars | Review created; tradesman avg\_rating recalculated and updated |  |
| T29 | Duplicate review prevented | Logged in as customer | Attempt to submit second review on same completed job | HTTP 409 returned; duplicate not created |  |
| T30 | Rating out of range rejected | Logged in as customer | Attempt POST /api/reviews with rating=0 | HTTP 400 returned; review not created |  |
| T31 | Review only possible on completed job | Logged in as customer | Attempt POST /api/reviews with job\_id of accepted job | HTTP 400 returned; review not created |  |
| T32 | Review visible on tradesman profile | After T28 | Navigate to reviewed tradesman's profile | New review text and star rating displayed in reviews section |  |
| T33 | avg\_rating denormalised correctly after review | After T28 | Check tradesman profile avg\_rating vs manual calculation | avg\_rating matches mathematical average of all reviews rounded to 1dp |  |
| **Access Control & Security** |  |  |  |  |  |
| T34 | Protected route redirect without JWT | Not logged in | Navigate directly to /search in browser | Redirected to /login |  |
| T35 | Protected API endpoint without JWT | Not logged in | Call GET /api/jobs/mine without Authorization header | HTTP 401 returned |  |
| T36 | Expired JWT handled gracefully | Logged in with expired token | Send request with expired token | HTTP 401 returned; Axios interceptor clears localStorage; redirected to /login |  |
| T37 | Customer cannot access tradesman-only data | Logged in as customer | Tradesman-specific job details are not exposed beyond their own jobs | My Jobs only shows customer's own sent requests |  |
| **UI, Usability & General** |  |  |  |  |  |
| T38 | Loading spinners display during API calls | Any authenticated page | Trigger data fetch (navigate to search); observe load state | Spinner displayed while data is loading |  |
| T39 | Responsive layout at mobile width | Any page | Resize browser to \< 700px width | Filter sidebar stacks above results; layout usable on mobile |  |
| T40 | Empty state on search with no results | Search page | Apply combination of filters that matches no tradesman | 'No tradesmen found' message displayed; no errors |  |
| T41 | Status badge colours correct | My Jobs page | Review jobs with different statuses | Pending=orange, Accepted=green, Declined=red, Completed=blue |  |
| T42 | Navigation links correct for each role | Both roles | Log in as customer, then as tradesman; check navbar links | Navigation items appropriate to role; no broken links |  |
| T43 | Health check endpoint responds | Any | GET /health | { status: 'ok', service: 'MasterLink API' } |  |

Table 12: Full Test Plan and Results (43 tests — 43 Pass, 0 Fail)

All 43 tests passed. No critical bugs remained at the time of final submission. Minor issues identified and resolved during testing included: the session hydration flash on page refresh (see Section 7.4), the Haversine degrees-to-radians conversion error (Section 7.4), and an edge case where the job status PATCH endpoint returned 403 for tradesmen due to incorrect ownership check ordering.

# **9\. Critical Review and Evaluation**

## **9.1 Overall Project Evaluation**

Overall, the project was a success. All Must Have and Should Have requirements were implemented and verified through testing. The team delivered a working, end-to-end proof-of-concept marketplace within the project timeline, covering authentication, search with advanced filtering and proximity sorting, a complete job lifecycle, and a verified review system. The codebase is well-structured, with clear separation of concerns across the three tiers and between individual route modules and their controllers.

The Agile methodology proved well-suited to the project's nature. By prioritising the database schema and authentication in early sprints, the team ensured a stable foundation before building features on top of it. The sprint structure also provided natural checkpoints to reassess the scope and deprioritise features (such as in-app messaging) that were planned but not feasible within the available time. The decision to use SQLite for the PoC — rather than spending time setting up and configuring a PostgreSQL server — was the right call and saved significant setup time at the cost of a known limitation.

In retrospect, one area where more upfront time could have been invested is input validation. Manual if-checks spread across multiple controller files made the codebase more verbose and inconsistent. Had a validation library such as Zod or Joi been adopted from the outset, validation rules would be centralised, easier to read, and less prone to inconsistency between endpoints.

## **9.2 Overall Product Evaluation**

The MasterLink product achieves its core aims: a homeowner in Dushanbe or Khujand can register, search for a specific type of tradesman, filter by price and rating, review the tradesman's profile and reviews, submit a job request, and leave a review after completion — all within a clean, responsive web interface. A tradesman can register, appear in search results, manage incoming requests, and build a verifiable review history.

The user interface successfully balances simplicity with feature richness. The search page — the most complex UI in the application — provides six filter controls and four sort options without feeling overwhelming, partly because the sidebar is collapsible and the defaults (no filters applied) show all results immediately. The colour-coded status system provides immediate visual feedback on job state without requiring the user to read status labels. The responsive design means the platform is usable on a smartphone browser, which is critical for the target market given Tajikistan's high mobile internet penetration.

The tradesman detail page provides sufficient information for an informed hiring decision: trade, city, years of experience, hourly rate, call-out fee, bio, and up to five recent reviews with star ratings and comments. This mirrors the core value proposition of the platform: transparency and social proof.

The product has genuine weaknesses as a PoC. The absence of in-app messaging is the most significant usability gap: after a job is accepted, the customer and tradesman must communicate off-platform to confirm arrival times, directions, and scope of work. The absence of push or email notifications means tradesmen have no prompt to check for new job requests. These are the two highest-priority items for a v2 release.

## **9.3 Project Limitations**

| Limitation | Detail | Future Mitigation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| SQLite not production-scalable | Single-file DB unsuitable for concurrent multi-user production traffic | Migrate to PostgreSQL with connection pooling (e.g. Supabase) |
| JWT in localStorage (XSS risk) | Accessible to JavaScript; XSS attack could steal tokens | Move to httpOnly cookies with CSRF protection |
| No email/SMS notifications | Tradesmen must poll the app manually for new requests | Integrate SendGrid (email) and Twilio (SMS) |
| No in-app messaging | Post-booking coordination must happen off-platform | WebSocket-based chat (Socket.io) or external service (Twilio Conversations) |
| No payment processing | No escrow; payment is entirely off-platform with no protection for either party | Integrate Stripe with escrow-style payment releases |
| No input validation library | Manual if-checks are inconsistent and verbose | Adopt Zod or Joi for structured schema-based validation |
| No rate limiting | Auth endpoints vulnerable to brute-force attacks | Apply express-rate-limit middleware to auth routes |
| No automated tests | All testing was manual; regressions not caught automatically | Add Jest unit tests \+ Supertest API integration tests |
| Geo-coordinates hardcoded | Tradesmen cannot set location via UI; proximity search uses seed data only | Integrate Google Maps Geocoding API; allow tradesman to set address during registration |
| No admin panel | No moderation of reviews, listings, or user accounts except direct DB access | Build role='admin' with dedicated management dashboard |
| Reviews limited to last 5 | GET /api/tradesmen/:id returns only 5 most recent reviews | Add pagination to reviews endpoint |

Table 13: Project Limitations and Mitigations

## **9.4 Future Development**

A commercially viable MasterLink product would require the following development priorities, roughly ordered by impact:

Phase 1 — Production Infrastructure: migrate the database from SQLite to PostgreSQL; move token storage from localStorage to httpOnly cookies; add rate limiting to authentication endpoints; implement a proper input validation library throughout the API.

Phase 2 — Core Missing Features: build in-app messaging (a WebSocket-based chat thread per job, visible to the customer and tradesman assigned to that job); add email notifications for key lifecycle events (new job request, acceptance, completion, new review) via SendGrid; integrate a payment provider (Stripe) with an escrow model where the customer pre-authorises payment at booking and it is released to the tradesman upon completion.

Phase 3 — Trust & Safety: implement identity verification for tradesman registration (document upload reviewed by admin); build an admin moderation panel covering user management, review moderation, and dispute resolution; add tradesman self-service profile editing.

Phase 4 — Growth Features: native iOS and Android mobile applications (React Native, sharing the same backend API); expand from Dushanbe and Khujand to additional Tajik cities; explore extension to neighbouring markets (Uzbekistan, Kyrgyzstan); add multi-language support (Tajik, Russian, English).

## **9.5 Own Learning Review**

This project provided the team with substantial learning across technical and project management dimensions.

Technically, the project deepened the team's understanding of full-stack JavaScript development — particularly the design of a REST API with multiple middleware layers, dynamic SQL query construction, and JWT-based authentication. The experience of building a relational database schema from a real domain problem (rather than a textbook exercise) gave tangible insight into normalisation trade-offs. The Haversine formula implementation was an unexpected opportunity to apply mathematical concepts to a practical geographic computing problem.

The frontend work solidified understanding of React's Context API, the React Router DOM v7 protected route pattern, and the Axios interceptor pattern for centralised authentication header management. Managing shared state across multiple pages through context (rather than prop-drilling) was a significant improvement on previous React projects.

From a project management perspective, the Agile sprint structure proved valuable — particularly the discipline of defining a MoSCoW priority list explicitly before development began. This prevented the temptation to implement attractive-but-non-essential features (such as avatar photo uploads) at the expense of core functionality. Git branch-based development with pull request reviews, even within a small team, caught multiple issues before they reached the main branch.

The most important meta-lesson was the value of building backend and database first, then frontend. In earlier individual projects, some team members had built the UI first and then struggled to retrofit the API to match it. The discipline of starting with the data model and API contract produced a cleaner, more cohesive system.

# **10\. Conclusion**

The home services sector in Tajikistan operates almost entirely on informal trust networks, leaving homeowners unable to compare tradesmen, verify credentials, or review previous work — and leaving skilled tradespeople unable to market themselves beyond their immediate personal circle. This is a genuine inefficiency with real costs for both parties.

MasterLink demonstrates that a digital marketplace can address this gap. The proof-of-concept delivers the full core value loop: a homeowner can find, evaluate, hire, and review a tradesman in Dushanbe or Khujand through a clean web interface, and a tradesman can manage their workload and build a verifiable professional reputation. All Must Have and Should Have functional requirements were implemented and passed testing. The system's architecture — a React SPA, Express.js REST API, and SQLite database — is well-structured and ready to evolve toward a production-grade system.

The competitive analysis confirmed that no equivalent platform exists for the Tajik market or, more broadly, for Central Asia. While TaskRabbit, Thumbtack, and Checkatrade serve similar needs in Western markets, none operates in Tajikistan or in any comparable post-Soviet Central Asian context. The specific challenges of the Tajik market — developing digital literacy, cash-based transaction habits, and the importance of personal trust — mean that a locally-developed, locally-branded platform has significant advantages over any hypothetical foreign entrant.

The project confirmed several key design decisions. The Haversine proximity sort is a meaningful differentiator in a geographically spread city. The denormalised rating approach keeps search queries fast without complex joins. The role-based access control model provides clean separation between customer and tradesman experiences. And the structured job lifecycle — pending, accepted, declined, completed — provides a foundation robust enough to support future features including in-app payment and automated notifications.

MasterLink is a technically sound, conceptually validated foundation. The path to a commercially viable product is clear: production database, payment processing, in-app messaging, and mobile applications. The team's recommendation is that this proof-of-concept merits continuation into a funded development phase targeting initial launch in Dushanbe.
